title: Viral Gastroenteritis
section: Infections Due to DNA and RNA Respiratory Viruses
authors: Umesh D. Parashar, Roger I. Glass

items.title: Global Impact of Viral Gastroenteritis
items.content: Acute infectious gastroenteritis is a significant global health issue, particularly affecting children in developing countries and the elderly with debilitating conditions. It leads to substantial morbidity and mortality worldwide.
keyPoints: Causes an estimated 0.5 million deaths annually among children in developing countries.
Responsible for up to 6–8% of all hospitalizations among children in industrialized nations, including the United States.
Elderly with debilitating health conditions are at high risk for severe complications and death.

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnostic Challenges of Viral Gastroenteritis
items.content: Viral gastroenteritis, primarily caused by RNA viruses but also occasionally by DNA viruses like adenovirus types 40 and 41, presents with symptoms such as vomiting, diarrhea, fever, nausea, abdominal cramps, anorexia, and malaise. Distinguishing viral from bacterial causes based on clinical features alone is challenging, necessitating laboratory confirmation.
keyPoints: Caused by several enteric viruses, predominantly RNA viruses.
Symptoms include acute onset of vomiting/diarrhea, fever, nausea, abdominal cramps, anorexia, and malaise.
Clinical distinction from bacterial gastroenteritis is difficult without laboratory tests.

items.title: Norovirus as a Leading Cause of Gastroenteritis
items.content: Noroviruses are the most common cause of gastroenteritis worldwide, especially in developed countries following rotavirus vaccination programs.
keyPoints: Noroviruses account for ~50% of all reported outbreaks of gastroenteritis in the United States.
They have become the leading cause of medically attended gastroenteritis in young children in some developed countries post-rotavirus vaccination implementation.

items.title: Transmission and Infectivity of Norovirus
items.content: Noroviruses are highly infectious, transmitted via the fecal-oral route, vomitus, aerosolization, contaminated fomites, and person-to-person contact.
keyPoints: A very small inoculum can be infectious.
Viral shedding occurs during acute illness, before symptom onset, and for weeks after resolution, especially in immunocompromised individuals.

items.title: Pathogenesis of Norovirus Infection
items.content: Noroviruses attach to carbohydrates similar to human histo-blood group antigens on the gastroduodenal epithelium, causing reversible lesions.
keyPoints: Attachment may involve carbohydrates present in individuals with the secretor phenotype.
Lesions include broadening and blunting of villi, vacuolization, crypt hyperplasia, and infiltration by polymorphonuclear neutrophils and lymphocytes.

items.subtopic: HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses
items.title: Immunity Paradox in Norwalk Virus Infection
items.content: Approximately 50% of individuals exposed to the Norwalk virus become ill, acquiring short-term immunity. Interestingly, higher preexisting antibody levels correlate with increased susceptibility upon rechallenge due to genetic predispositions affecting HBGA phenotypes.
keyPoints: Around 50% of challenged persons develop illness and short-term immunity.
Higher preexisting antibodies paradoxically increase susceptibility on rechallenge.
Genetic predisposition related to specific HBGA phenotypes influences susceptibility.
items.reference: Viral Gastroenteritis · HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses

items.subtopic: HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses
items.title: Diagnostic Approaches for Norovirus
items.content: PCR assays are used to detect norovirus in stool and vomitus, while enzyme immunoassays using virus-like particles have limited sensitivity but are useful during outbreaks.
keyPoints: PCR-based detection assays are increasingly used clinically.
VLP-based EIAs have limited clinical sensitivity but are useful in outbreak settings.
items.reference: Viral Gastroenteritis · HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses

items.subtopic: HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses
items.title: Norovirus Vaccine Efficacy
items.content: Vaccines based on VLPs show promise, with a monovalent GI.1 vaccine showing 47% efficacy intranasally and a bivalent GI.1/GI.4 vaccine demonstrating 61.8% efficacy against moderate/severe gastroenteritis in a field study.
keyPoints: Monovalent GI.1 VLP vaccine showed 47% efficacy when administered intranasally.
Bivalent GI.1/GI.4 VLP vaccine demonstrated 61.8% efficacy for moderate/severe norovirus acute gastroenteritis in a field study.
items.reference: Viral Gastroenteritis · HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses

items.title: Epidemiology of Rotavirus Infections
items.content: Rotavirus is a leading cause of diarrheal death among children in developing countries, with the highest mortality rates observed in sub-Saharan Africa and southern Asia. The disease typically affects children under five years old, with first symptomatic infections occurring after 3 months of age. Reinfections are common but tend to be less severe.
keyPoints: Nearly all children worldwide are infected by age 3–5.
High mortality rates in sub-Saharan Africa and southern Asia due to suboptimal access to hydration therapy.
First symptomatic infections occur after 3 months of age, peaking at 4–23 months.
Reinfections decrease in severity with each occurrence.

items.title: Transmission and Seasonality of Rotavirus
items.content: Rotavirus is transmitted predominantly through the fecal-oral route, but can also spread via respiratory secretions, person-to-person contact, or contaminated surfaces. In temperate regions, rotavirus season peaks during cooler months, while in tropical settings it occurs year-round with less pronounced seasonal variation.
keyPoints: Transmission is primarily fecal-oral.
Seasonal peaks occur in fall and winter in temperate areas; year-round transmission in tropical settings.
Pre-vaccine pattern showed migration from the Southwest to Northeast U.S. during cooler months.

items.title: Pathogenesis of Rotavirus-Induced Diarrhea
items.content: Rotaviruses infect and destroy mature enterocytes in the proximal small intestine, leading to secretory diarrhea. The loss of absorptive epithelium and proliferation of crypt cells contribute to this condition. NSP4, a nonstructural protein, acts as an enterotoxin affecting cell function and permeability.
keyPoints: Infects mature enterocytes in the proximal small intestine.
Results in secretory diarrhea due to loss of absorptive epithelium.
NSP4 functions as an enterotoxin altering cell function and permeability.

items.title: Clinical Spectrum and Severity of Rotavirus Infection
items.content: Rotavirus infection can range from subclinical to severe gastroenteritis, often leading to life-threatening dehydration. The illness typically begins abruptly after an incubation period of 1–3 days, with vomiting frequently preceding diarrhea. Fever >39°C may occur in up to one-third of patients. Gastrointestinal symptoms generally resolve within 3–7 days.
keyPoints: Incubation period: 1–3 days
Symptoms onset: abrupt, vomiting often precedes diarrhea
Fever occurrence: >39°C in up to one-third of patients
Stool characteristics: loose and watery, rarely contain red or white cells
Resolution time for symptoms: 3–7 days

items.title: Diagnosis and Immunity of Rotavirus Infection
items.content: Rotavirus infection is challenging to distinguish from other enteric viruses based solely on clinical presentation. Diagnosis can be confirmed by detecting large quantities of virus in feces using EIAs or viral RNA detection techniques like PCR. Immunity against rotavirus correlates with secretory IgA antibodies, which provide temporary protection. Each subsequent infection enhances immunity, reducing disease severity over time.
keyPoints: Diagnosis methods: EIAs, gel electrophoresis, probe hybridization, PCR
Immunity correlation: presence of virus-specific secretory IgA antibodies
Protection duration: temporary due to short-lived IgA production at intestinal surface
Reinfection impact: progressively greater immunity with each infection

items.title: Management of Rotavirus Dehydration
items.content: Oral rehydration is preferred for treating rotavirus dehydration. However, IV fluids are necessary for severely dehydrated patients or those unable to tolerate oral therapy due to frequent vomiting.
keyPoints: Oral rehydration is the primary treatment method.
IV fluid replacement required for severe dehydration or inability to tolerate oral therapy.

items.title: Role of Rotavirus Vaccination in Public Health
items.content: The introduction of rotavirus vaccines has significantly reduced hospitalizations and emergency visits due to rotavirus. Herd immunity benefits even those who are unvaccinated or ineligible for vaccination.
keyPoints: RotaTeq and Rotarix show 85–98% efficacy against severe disease.
>70–80% decline in hospitalizations and ED visits post-vaccine introduction.
Herd protection observed, reducing rotavirus transmission community-wide.

items.title: Efficacy of Rotavirus Vaccines in Developing Countries
items.content: While the efficacy of rotavirus vaccines is moderate (50–65%) in developing countries compared to industrialized nations, their use still provides substantial public health benefits.
keyPoints: Moderate vaccine efficacy (50–65%) in low-income settings.
Substantial public health benefits despite modest efficacy.
WHO prequalification of Rotavac and Rotasiil for global procurement.

items.question: Which of the following groups is most affected by severe complications and death from acute gastroenteritis?
options: Elderly persons with debilitating health conditions
items.explanation: Elderly persons, especially those with debilitating health conditions, are at risk of severe complications and death from acute gastroenteritis.

items.question: What is the primary cause of viral gastroenteritis?
items.explanation: Most viral gastroenteritis is caused by RNA viruses.

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is NOT typically associated with viral gastroenteritis?
items.explanation: Viral gastroenteritis is characterized by vomiting, diarrhea, fever, nausea, abdominal cramps, anorexia, and malaise, but not typically skin rash.

items.question: Which of the following best describes the molecular characteristics of human caliciviruses?
options: They have a double-stranded DNA genome.
They possess a single, positive-strand RNA genome approximately 7.5 kb in length.
Their virion-associated protein has a molecular mass of 120 kDa.
They are enveloped viruses with distinct surface features.
items.explanation: Human caliciviruses have a single, positive-strand RNA genome approximately 7.5 kb in length and possess a virion-associated protein with a molecular mass of 60 kDa.

items.question: Which genogroups of noroviruses are known to infect humans?
items.explanation: Of the 10 recognized norovirus genogroups in humans and animals, 35 different genotypes belonging to 5 genogroups (GI, GII, GIV, GVIII, and GIX) are known to infect humans.

items.question: What is the primary mode of transmission for noroviruses?
options: Fecal-oral route and contact with vomitus
items.explanation: Norovirus is transmitted predominantly by the fecal-oral route but is also present in vomitus.

items.question: Which age group is primarily affected by sapoviruses causing gastroenteritis?
items.explanation: Sapoviruses primarily cause gastroenteritis in children.

items.subtopic: HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses
items.question: Which factor influences susceptibility to norovirus infection according to early human volunteer studies?
options: Higher levels of preexisting antibody correlate with increased susceptibility.
Lower levels of preexisting antibody correlate with decreased susceptibility.
Genetic predisposition has no impact on susceptibility.
items.explanation: Persons with higher levels of preexisting antibody to Norwalk virus were more susceptible to illness upon rechallenge, explained by genetic predisposition and specific HBGA phenotypes.
items.reference: Viral Gastroenteritis · HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses

items.subtopic: HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses
items.question: What is the primary method for diagnosing norovirus infection in clinical settings?
options: Enzyme immunoassays (EIAs) are the most sensitive.
Virus-like particle assays are used exclusively.
items.explanation: PCR-based detection assays for norovirus are increasingly used in clinical settings due to their ability to detect virus in stool and vomitus.
items.reference: Viral Gastroenteritis · HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses

items.subtopic: HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses
items.question: What is the role of enzyme immunoassays (EIAs) in norovirus detection?
options: They are highly sensitive and useful for individual diagnosis.
They have limited sensitivity but are useful in outbreak settings.
They can detect all enteric pathogens simultaneously.
items.explanation: Commercial EIA kits for detection in stool have limited sensitivity and usefulness, mainly in outbreaks where many specimens are tested.
items.reference: Viral Gastroenteritis · HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses

items.subtopic: HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses
items.question: What was the reported vaccine efficacy for moderate/severe norovirus acute gastroenteritis in a field study involving U.S. Navy recruits?
items.explanation: The vaccine efficacy for moderate/severe norovirus acute gastroenteritis due to any type was reported as 61.8% with a confidence interval of 20.8–81.6% in the field study.
items.reference: Viral Gastroenteritis · HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses

items.question: Which viral protein is the target of commercial immunoassays for rotaviruses and determines their group specificity?
items.explanation: Viral protein 6 (VP6) is the major structural protein targeted by commercial immunoassays for rotaviruses and determines their group specificity.

items.question: What are the two outer-capsid proteins of rotavirus that determine serotype specificity?
items.explanation: The two outer-capsid proteins, VP7 (G-protein) and VP4 (P-protein), determine serotype specificity of rotaviruses.

items.question: Which groups of rotavirus primarily cause human illness?
items.explanation: Human illness is caused primarily by group A rotaviruses and to a much lesser extent by groups B and C.

items.question: What role does the segmented genome of rotavirus play in its evolution?
options: It facilitates genetic reassortment during co-infection
items.explanation: The segmented genome of rotavirus allows genetic reassortment, which plays a role in its evolution.

items.question: Which symptom frequently precedes the onset of diarrhea in rotavirus infection?
items.explanation: Vomiting frequently precedes the onset of diarrhea in rotavirus infection.

items.question: What is the typical duration for gastrointestinal symptoms to resolve in rotavirus infection?
items.explanation: Gastrointestinal symptoms generally resolve in 3–7 days.

items.question: Which antibody is correlated with protection against rotavirus disease?
items.explanation: Protection against rotavirus disease is correlated with the presence of virus-specific secretory IgA antibodies in the intestine.

items.question: Which of the following treatments is recommended for patients with severe dehydration due to rotavirus infection who cannot tolerate oral rehydration?
items.explanation: Intravenous (IV) fluid replacement is recommended for patients with severe dehydration who cannot tolerate oral therapy.

items.question: What was the reason for withdrawing the first rotavirus vaccine licensed in the United States?
items.explanation: The first rotavirus vaccine was withdrawn due to its link with a low incidence of intussusception.

items.question: What is the efficacy range of RotaTeq and Rotarix vaccines against severe rotavirus disease as reported from clinical trials?
items.explanation: The efficacy of RotaTeq and Rotarix against severe rotavirus disease was reported to be between 85–98%.

items.question: Which organization recommended the use of rotavirus vaccines worldwide in April 2009?
options: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
items.explanation: The World Health Organization (WHO) recommended the use of rotavirus vaccines worldwide in April 2009.

items.statement: Acute infectious gastroenteritis is rarely fatal among healthy young adults.
items.explanation: Among healthy young adults, acute gastroenteritis is rarely fatal.

items.statement: Bacterial agents are the primary cause of viral gastroenteritis.
items.explanation: Most viral gastroenteritis is caused by RNA viruses, not bacterial agents.

items.statement: Norovirus is the leading cause of medically attended gastroenteritis in young children in developed countries following rotavirus vaccination programs.
items.explanation: In developed countries, with the decline in severe rotavirus disease following implementation of a rotavirus vaccination program, norovirus has become the leading cause of medically attended gastroenteritis in young children.

items.statement: Histopathologic changes are observed in both the stomach and colon during human calicivirus infection.
items.explanation: No histopathologic changes are seen in the stomach or colon, but gastric motor function is delayed.

items.subtopic: HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses
items.statement: Specific HBGA phenotypes do not influence susceptibility to norovirus infection.
items.explanation: Specific HBGA phenotypes are known to influence susceptibility to norovirus infection.
items.reference: Viral Gastroenteritis · HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses

items.subtopic: HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses
items.statement: No specific antiviral therapy is available for norovirus infections.
items.explanation: The passage states that no specific antiviral therapy is currently available for norovirus infections.
items.reference: Viral Gastroenteritis · HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses

items.statement: Rotavirus disease is more seasonal and occurs at an older age in developing countries compared to industrialized ones.
items.explanation: In developing countries, rotavirus disease occurs at a younger age, is less seasonal, and is often more severe.

items.statement: Rotaviruses are transmitted predominantly through the fecal-oral route.
items.explanation: The virus is primarily transmitted through the fecal-oral route.

items.statement: Rotavirus infection has been confirmed to cause sudden infant death syndrome.
items.explanation: No causal relationship has been confirmed between rotavirus infection and sudden infant death syndrome.

items.statement: Immunosuppressed individuals are at increased risk for severe or fatal rotavirus disease.
items.explanation: Persons who are immunosuppressed, such as those undergoing bone marrow transplantation, are at risk for severe or even fatal rotavirus disease.

items.statement: Antibiotics are a recommended treatment for rotavirus infections.
items.explanation: Antibiotics should be avoided in the treatment of rotavirus infections.

items.statement: The efficacy of oral rotavirus vaccines is generally higher in developing countries compared to industrialized nations.
items.explanation: The efficacy of oral rotavirus vaccines is moderate (50–65%) in developing countries, which is lower than that observed in industrialized countries.

items.assertion: Acute infectious gastroenteritis incurs substantial medical and social costs.
items.reason: This includes time lost from work among healthy young adults.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why acute gastroenteritis incurs substantial costs.

items.assertion: Norovirus causes reversible lesions in the upper jejunum.
items.reason: These lesions are associated with malabsorption of carbohydrates and fats due to changes in the intestinal lining.
items.explanation: After infection, reversible lesions are noted in the upper jejunum, which are associated with malabsorption of carbohydrates and fats.

items.assertion: Norovirus is transmitted through respiratory droplets.
items.reason: The virus can be aerosolized from vomitus, leading to transmission by inhalation.
items.explanation: While norovirus can be aerosolized, its primary mode of transmission is the fecal-oral route and contact with vomitus.

items.subtopic: HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses
items.assertion: Functional antibodies that block norovirus binding to HBGAs correlate with protective immunity.
items.reason: Blocking antibodies neutralize norovirus grown in vitro in human intestinal enteroid cell-based systems.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not directly explain the correlation between functional antibodies and protective immunity.
items.reference: Viral Gastroenteritis · HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses

items.subtopic: HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses
items.assertion: Epidemic prevention of norovirus relies solely on immunoprophylaxis.
items.reason: Immunoprophylaxis is not clearly established due to the lack of long-term immunity from natural disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because epidemic prevention involves multiple measures, while the reason is true regarding the unclear role of immunoprophylaxis.
items.reference: Viral Gastroenteritis · HUMAN CALICIVIRUSES (part 2) | Infections with Norwalk and Related Human Caliciviruses

items.assertion: Rotavirus infections are less severe with each subsequent infection.
items.reason: Reinfections are common, and the severity of disease decreases with each repeat infection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why reinfections tend to be less severe.

items.assertion: Rotavirus disease occurs predominantly during cooler months in temperate settings.
items.reason: This pattern is due to state-specific differences in birth rates influencing the accumulation of susceptible infants.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not directly explain why rotavirus peaks during cooler months.

items.assertion: Routine use of rotavirus vaccines has led to a significant decline in hospitalizations and emergency department visits for rotavirus disease.
items.reason: The introduction of rotavirus vaccines has resulted in >70–80% reduction in such cases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: The first rotavirus vaccine was withdrawn due to its high cost.
items.reason: It was linked with a low incidence of intussusception.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true; the vaccine was withdrawn because it was linked to intussusception.
